metadata.title: Draft and Stress-Test a DAG Defense with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I want to make my DAG defense survive an actual adversary, not just look tidy. Take a causal DAG I hand you and interrogate it edge by edge, the way a hostile reviewer would. Specifically: (1) for each arrow, state the mechanism that justifies its direction and name the strongest reversed-causation objection, (2) identify any missing common cause that would collapse my identification, and (3) rank the three edges most likely to break under scrutiny so I know where to gather evidence first.

props.command: I want to make my DAG defense survive an actual adversary, not just look tidy. Take a causal DAG I hand you and interrogate it edge by edge, the way a hostile reviewer would. Specifically: (1) for each arrow, state the mechanism that justifies its direction and name the strongest reversed-causation objection, (2) identify any missing common cause that would collapse my identification, and (3) rank the three edges most likely to break under scrutiny so I know where to gather evidence first.
props.segment: Draft and Stress-Test a DAG Defense

beats.narration: Claude drafts a professional-sounding DAG defense — then you find the fabricated citation hiding in paragraph two.
A DAG defense is not about defending your assumptions — it is about making them visible enough that someone else can attack them. Claude can draft the structure; the student does the fact-check.
We ask Claude to draft a three-part DAG defense for a healthcare DAG: justify each arrow, rank missing arrows by bias direction, and name two unmeasured confounders.

props.command:
claude "Draft a three-part DAG defense:
  DAG: treatment→outcome, age→both,
       severity→treatment, severity→outcome
  (1) justify each arrow mechanistically
  (2) rank 3 missing arrows by bias direction
  (3) name 2 unmeasured confounders
  Use academic-register language."

beats.narration: Claude returns a three-part defense. Part one justifies each arrow. Part two ranks missing arrows. Part three names two confounders. One citation in part one — open it. Does it exist?

props.code:
# dag_defense.md — Claude output (excerpt)

## Part 1: Arrow Justifications
severity→treatment: high-severity patients
  receive more aggressive treatment
  (cf. Brookhart et al. 2006, NEJM) ← verify
age→outcome: older patients have higher
  baseline mortality (mechanistic)
treatment→outcome: direct biological
  mechanism via [pathway]

## Part 2: Missing Arrows (bias direction)
1. comorbidity→outcome  (upward bias if omitted)
2. provider_skill→treatment (upward bias)
3. SES→both              (downward bias)

## Part 3: Unmeasured Confounders
1. patient_preference (→treatment and outcome)
2. facility_quality   (→treatment and outcome)

beats.narration: The fact-check log: Brookhart et al. 2006 in NEJM — verify this. The actual Brookhart citation is from Epidemiology 2006, not NEJM. Claude placed a real author in a wrong journal. The arrow justifications are mechanistic and defensible. The confounder bias directions are correct.
We ask Claude to rewrite the defense in plain language for a non-specialist audience and verify the structural claims are preserved without inventing new ones.

props.command:
claude "Rewrite the DAG defense in plain language
  for a non-specialist audience.
  Preserve all structural claims.
  Do not add new causal claims."

beats.narration: The plain-language version preserves the three-part structure. Sicker patients get more treatment — that is the severity arrow. Older patients do worse — the age arrow. Two things we did not measure that could affect both treatment choice and outcome — the unmeasured confounders. No new claims added.

The DAG defense has three parts: justify each arrow mechanistically, rank the most dangerous missing arrows by bias direction, and name the unmeasured confounders. Claude drafts it — you fact-check the citations and verify the bias-direction reasoning.
Next: propose and critique a confounder set — find the two that are actually mediators, and the one that is a collider in disguise.
Like and subscribe for more causal reasoning with Claude.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Draft and Stress-Test a DAG Defense with Claude] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
